attributes.stem: A 25-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic after his parent was recently diagnosed with hereditary haemorrhagic telangiectasia. He has experienced occasional epistaxis since childhood but is otherwise well. He has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits. There are several small telangiectasias on his lips and tongue.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following investigations is most appropriate?
attributes.explanation:
This patient has likely inherited hereditary haemorrhagic telangiectasia (HHT), also known as Osler-Weber-Rendu syndrome. HHT is an autosomal dominant disorder characterised by abnormal blood vessel formation leading to arteriovenous malformations (AVMs) and mucocutaneous telangiectasias. The diagnosis is based on the Curaçao criteria: spontaneous recurrent epistaxis, multiple telangiectasias, visceral lesions, and family history. Gastrointestinal bleeding and iron deficiency anaemia are also frequently seen. AVMs most commonly affect the lungs (over 50% of patients), liver (30-70% of patients) and brain (10-15% of patients). Asymptomatic patients with HHT are recommended to undergo screening for iron deficiency and pulmonary AVMs (through echocardiography +/- CT imaging); screening for hepatic and cerebral AVMs may also be considered.

Iron studies are indicated as chronic blood loss from epistaxis and gastrointestinal telangiectasias frequently leads to iron deficiency anaemia in HHT patients. Early detection and treatment of iron deficiency can prevent complications and improve quality of life.

CT paranasal sinuses is not routinely indicated for HHT patients unless there are specific concerns about severe epistaxis requiring surgical intervention. The management of epistaxis typically begins with conservative measures such as humidification and topical treatments.

Gastroscopy and colonoscopy is not routinely recommended for screening in HHT unless there are symptoms suggesting significant gastrointestinal bleeding, which typically presents later in life. Gastrointestinal telangiectasias are present in about 40% of patients but are often asymptomatic.

CT brain is not the preferred modality for screening for cerebral AVMs in HHT. MRI is more sensitive for detecting small AVMs and does not involve radiation exposure. Furthermore, the screening of cerebral AVMs is a complex issue as the harms of screening may outweigh the benefits, and should be carefully discussed with the patient.

Liver biopsy is not indicated for screening in HHT and could be dangerous due to the risk of bleeding. If hepatic AVMs are suspected, non-invasive imaging such as CT or MRI would be more appropriate.
learningPoints: Iron deficiency is common in hereditary haemorrhagic telangiectasia due to chronic blood loss and should be screened for regularly.
Screening for AVMs, particularly pulmonary, should also be considered
references: Faughnan ME, Mager JJ, Hetts SW, et al. Second International Guidelines for the Diagnosis and Management of Hereditary Hemorrhagic Telangiectasia. Ann Intern Med. 2020;173(12):989-1001. Available at: https://hhtguidelines.org/wp-content/uploads/2020/09/Second-International-HHT-Guidelines-Annals-2020.pdf
